method_atom: A delegated-computation protocol in which a resource-limited principal offloads a computation to a powerful but untrusted provider while keeping its inputs, outputs, and the computation itself perfectly hidden: the principal only needs to emit randomly chosen states drawn from a small fixed set, then steers the computation step by step through adaptive two-way classical messaging in which each next instruction depends on prior reported outcomes. The randomization acts as a one-time encoding that blinds the provider, and an interleaved authentication/challenge layer lets the principal detect a cheating provider and tolerate faults; the same construction extends to multiple non-communicating providers coordinated by a purely classical principal, yielding a verifiable interactive-proof structure.
object_atom: Quantum computation carried out by a quantum server for a client who has no quantum computational power beyond preparing and sending single qubits, exploiting measurement-based quantum computing to separate its quantum and classical parts; the two-prover generalization shows every problem in BQP has an entangled interactive proof with a classical verifier.
brief_reason: What transfers is the blind, verifiable delegation-of-computation machinery (randomized one-time encoding plus adaptive step-driving and cheat-detecting authentication); what is domain-specific is the qubits, measurement-based quantum computing, and BQP/entangled-prover setting.
